title: Stamping texture into clay
subtitle: Pressing stamps, found objects, and textiles into soft clay for surface pattern
excerpt: Stamping is the simplest surface decoration technique for clay: press an object into soft clay and remove it, leaving a negative impression. Stamps, found objects, leaves, and textiles all work. The impression is permanent once the clay dries. The technique works at the soft stage rather than leather-hard, before rolling is complete. No kiln or wheel is needed.
sourceNotes: Clay stamping technique. Standard hand-building surface decoration. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: into clay works on any clay body at any stage from fresh off the bag to leather-hard, though the freshest clay gives the deepest, cleanest impression. On a fresh

, texture can be added by rolling, placing the stamp under or over the clay before rolling across it. On a formed piece, stamps are pressed into the surface by hand. Almost any object with a flat face and a repeating or textured surface can be used as a stamp.

attrs.body: Shape a thick slab of clay into a handle-like form. Carve a pattern into the flat pressing face while the clay is leather-hard. Allow to bone-dry, this makes a serviceable stamp for air-dry clay. For a more durable stamp, the clay one can be used as a master to press a plaster stamp, which is harder and lasts longer.
attrs.title: Making a stamp from clay

items.name: Stamps, leaves, lace, or found objects for texture

content.text: Method 1: Rolling texture into a fresh slab

Place a piece of lace, open-weave fabric, or a flat stamp face-down on the canvas board. Lay the clay block on top and roll from the top as normal, using guide rails. The texture is pressed into the underside of the slab as the rolling pin compresses the clay. Peel the clay off the texture object and flip it over: the textured side is now face-up. This method gives an all-over, consistent texture across a large area.
Method 2: Pressing a stamp by hand

Hold the stamp face-down over the clay surface. Press straight down with firm, even pressure and release without rocking the stamp sideways, rocking blurs the impression edges. For a stamp with a handle, hold the handle and push directly down. For a flat object (leaf, coin, key), press with the heel of the hand or roll once with the rolling pin.
What makes a good stamping object

Good stamps: pottery stamps with a carved face; leaves with prominent veins; lace; open-weave hessian or burlap; carved erasers; carved hardwood blocks; buttons. Objects that do not work well: objects with undercuts that trap clay when lifted; very smooth surfaces with no relief; soft objects that deform under pressure.

For a repeating stamp pattern, press the stamp and mark the edge of the impression with a light needle tool scratch before repositioning. Align the next press to the scratch mark. A systematic repeat, rows at even intervals, requires more planning than a scattered impression but reads more clearly at a distance.

items.fix: Wipe the stamp face with a dry cloth. If the clay is very fresh, allow to firm for 15 minutes before stamping. A very light dusting of cornflour on the stamp face helps with stubborn sticking.
items.cause: Stamp surface not smooth or the clay too wet.
items.symptom: Stamp sticks and lifts the clay when removed

items.fix: Press with more force. The clay should compress visibly under the stamp. If the clay is at leather-hard, it is past the ideal stamping window for most stamps, use a sgraffito tool to carve a pattern instead.
items.cause: Pressure was too light or the clay surface was too stiff.
